title: Southern fried chicken
subtitle: Buttermilk-brined chicken pieces with a crisp, seasoned crust
excerpt: Bone-in chicken pieces soaked overnight in seasoned buttermilk, dredged in spiced flour, and deep-fried until the crust is shatteringly crisp and deeply golden. The brine tenderises and seasons throughout; the double-dredge builds a thick, craggy crust. This is the technique, not the approximation.
sourceNotes: Southern fried chicken is rooted in the African American cooking tradition of the American South, with the seasoned buttermilk brine and the spiced-flour coating forming a technique documented from the nineteenth century onward. It became one of the defining dishes of Southern cuisine and, through its commercialisation by fast-food chains and others from the mid-twentieth century, one of the most widely recognised American foods globally. The home version predates and exceeds all commercial versions.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The chicken must brine for at least 8 hours — overnight is ideal.

content.text: There are three things that separate good fried chicken from mediocre fried chicken: the brine, the crust, and the oil temperature. The brine is overnight buttermilk, which tenderises the meat and the lactic acid in the buttermilk seasons deeply through the piece, not just the surface. The crust is a double dredge: flour, back into the buttermilk, flour again, which builds a thick, rough coating with the craggy texture that makes it properly crisp rather than just coated.

The oil temperature is 175°C and must be maintained throughout. If the temperature drops too low, the crust absorbs fat before it sets and the chicken steams inside instead of frying. Use a thermometer. Fry in batches, never crowding the pan, and allow the oil to return to temperature between batches. The finished internal temperature for chicken thighs and drumsticks is 75°C at the thickest point.

Combine the {{buttermilk}} of buttermilk with 2 tsp {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt, 1 tsp {{black-pepper}} of black pepper, and {{louisiana-hot-sauce}} of hot sauce. Submerge the {{chicken-thigh}} chicken pieces in the brine, cover, and refrigerate for at least 8 hours.

Mix the {{plain-flour}} of plain flour with {{paprika-sweet}} of paprika, {{garlic-powder}} of garlic powder, {{onion-powder}} of onion powder, {{cayenne}} of cayenne, 1 tsp salt, and 1 tsp pepper in a wide bowl. Remove the chicken from the brine — do not shake off the buttermilk clinging to it. Dredge each piece in the seasoned flour, pressing firmly so it adheres. Dip briefly back into the buttermilk, then dredge again in the flour, pressing hard to create a thick, rough crust. Set on a rack and rest for 10 minutes.

Pour neutral oil into the cast-iron skillet to a depth of 4 cm and heat to 175°C. Fry the chicken in batches of 2 to 3 pieces, skin-side down, for 8 to 10 minutes without moving. Flip and fry for a further 7 to 8 minutes. The crust should be a deep mahogany brown and the internal temperature at the thickest point should read 75°C. Drain on a rack — not kitchen paper, which traps steam. Rest for 5 minutes before serving.

items.cause: Double-dredge not pressed firmly enough, or chicken moved too early
items.fix: Press the flour onto the chicken with real force; do not move the pieces for at least 7 minutes — the crust needs time to set and release naturally

items.symptom: Crust is dark but chicken is not cooked through
items.cause: Oil too hot; crust coloured before heat reached the bone
items.fix: Maintain 175°C strictly; if the crust is darkening too fast, reduce the heat slightly and finish in a 180°C oven

items.symptom: Crust is greasy and not crisp
items.cause: Oil temperature dropped too low when chicken was added
items.fix: Fry in small batches; cold chicken drops the oil temperature — allow it to return to 175°C before each batch
Brine for no more than 24 hours; remove at 75°C internal temperature, which leaves the meat safely cooked but still juicy

content.text: Southern fried chicken is one of the great dishes of the African American cooking tradition, developed in the American South over generations by cooks who combined West African frying techniques with the American pantry to produce a preparation of remarkable quality. The buttermilk brine, the spiced coating, and the deep-frying in cast iron are all hallmarks of this tradition. The dish was popularised globally through commercial fast-food operations from the mid-twentieth century, none of which captured what the original preparation actually is. The home version (brined overnight, double-dredged, fried at the right temperature in a cast-iron skillet) is worth understanding on its own terms.
